title: Where private insurance and clinical urgency sometimes pull against each other
caption:
Most of the private patients we see are insured, and the insurance system works well most of the time. But there is a tension I think is worth naming honestly, because it affects patients.

Private medical insurance involves a pre-authorisation step before treatment is approved. For planned, non-urgent care, that process is reasonable and works fine. Where it becomes difficult is when a condition is time-sensitive.

In ophthalmology, some conditions do not wait. The interval between recognising a problem and treating it can affect the outcome. When the approval process and the clinical clock are pulling in different directions, that is a hard position for everyone, the patient most of all.

I am not blaming anyone for this. Pre-authorisation exists for sensible reasons, and the people administering it are doing their jobs. But I do think the system, as a whole, could do better at recognising when ophthalmic urgency means the usual timelines need to flex.

What we do in the meantime is manage it actively for the patient: we push, we document the urgency clearly, and where a delay would be clinically unsafe we make sure the patient still gets seen. That is part of our job.

For colleagues who work across insured care: where have you found the process and the clinical need pulling against each other, and what helps?

title: Why I do not fully trust patient satisfaction scores, even good ones
caption:
Patient satisfaction scores are one of the most quoted numbers in healthcare. I pay attention to ours. But I have learned not to trust them completely, even when they are good. Especially when they are good.

Here is what a satisfaction score does not capture.

It does not capture the patient who was quietly unhappy and simply did not come back. They do not fill in the survey. They just go elsewhere, and the number stays high without you ever learning why.

It does not always distinguish between a patient who felt well looked after and a patient who got the best clinical outcome. Those usually go together, but not always. Someone can have a warm, reassuring experience and still not have had the result they should have had. A high satisfaction score can hide that.

And it rewards the things that are easy to feel, like friendliness and a nice waiting room, more reliably than the things that are hard to judge, like whether the clinical decision was the right one.

None of this means the score is worthless. A consistently poor score would tell me something is wrong. But a good score does not tell me everything is right. It tells me people were happy, which is necessary but not sufficient.

What I actually watch is the harder stuff: outcomes, complications, the patients who needed a second intervention, the feedback that did not come wrapped in a compliment. Those are less flattering and more useful.

For others who run a service: which number do you trust least, even when it looks good?

title: The question I ask every consultant before they join us
caption:
There is one conversation I have with every consultant before they join CES. The clinical credentials matter, of course, and we check them carefully. But the credentials are not what the conversation is about.

What I am really trying to understand is how they handle the moment when the honest answer and the profitable answer are not the same.

In private healthcare, there is always a quieter version of every consultation, the one where a patient could be sold something they do not really need. A premium lens that will not change much for them. A procedure that could reasonably wait. A patient who, told the truth, would walk away without booking anything.

So I ask, in one way or another: what do you do in that moment?

The answer I am listening for is not clever. It is simply someone who finds the question easy, because for them there was never really a choice. The patient gets the honest answer, even when the honest answer is "you do not need this," or "not yet," or "this is not the right thing for you."

I would rather have a consultant who sends a patient away without booking than one who is comfortable selling them something they do not need. The first builds a practice that lasts. The second does not, and it should not.

That is the conversation. It tells me more than any CV.

For others who hire in a field where trust is the product: what is the one thing you have to know about someone before you bring them in?

title: What good community eye care looks like when the pathway works
caption:

For instance: a community optometrist notices a subtle change at a routine sight test, something that does not quite fit. They refer appropriately. The patient is assessed, the problem is confirmed early, and it is treated before it becomes serious. Keep all patient details non-identifiable, no age plus condition plus location combination, nothing distinctive.]

What strikes me about cases like this is that no single person was the hero. The optometrist was paying attention and knew when to refer. The referral pathway did its job. By the time the patient reached us, the groundwork had been done, and our part was almost the easy bit.

That is what good community eye care looks like. It is not one brilliant clinician. It is a chain of people each doing their part well, and a system that lets the patient move through it without falling between the gaps.

I think we undervalue this. The dramatic saves get the attention, but most sight is protected quietly, by an optometrist who noticed something and a pathway that worked. The unglamorous version is the one worth celebrating.

title: The one change I would make to the eye care referral pathway
caption:
Every week I see the same thing, and it is the one part of the eye care pathway I would change if I could.

It is not a lack of skill or care anywhere in the system. It is that the path between someone first noticing a problem and reaching the right specialist is often longer and less direct than it needs to be.

A patient notices a change. They wait, because they are not sure it is serious. They see one person, who refers them to another, who refers them on again. Each step is reasonable on its own. Added together, they can cost time that, for some conditions, matters.

The change I would make is simple to say and hard to do: make it easier for the person who first sees the patient, often a community optometrist, to get them to the right specialist directly when the signs warrant it. Fewer steps. Less waiting between them.

I am not blaming anyone for the current system. It evolved for sensible reasons and the people in it work hard. But if I could change one thing, it would be the number of stops a patient makes between noticing something and being seen by the person who can help.

For colleagues across eye care: if you could change one thing about the pathway, what would it be?

title: Why corneal transplantation is one of the most technically demanding procedures in ophthalmology
caption:
Corneal transplantation has changed dramatically in the past decade. We have moved away from full-thickness grafts towards selective lamellar procedures that transplant only the diseased layer of the cornea rather than the entire structure.

DMEK (Descemet Membrane Endothelial Keratoplasty) is the most technically refined of these. The tissue being transplanted is a single cell layer, approximately 10 to 15 microns thick. To put that in context, a human hair is around 70 microns wide. The graft has no structural rigidity. It must be inserted into the eye through a small incision, unfolded without damage and positioned precisely against the recipient cornea using an air bubble.

The learning curve is steep. Outcomes in experienced hands are significantly better than in lower-volume centres. For patients with Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy or bullous keratopathy, this distinction matters.

I completed my doctoral research on corneal endothelial cell biology before specialising in transplantation. That foundation changes how I think about every operative decision, not just the technical steps but the cellular logic behind them.

If you are an optometrist or GP seeing patients with progressive corneal oedema or unexplained visual decline that does not respond to refraction, I am happy to discuss cases informally before a formal referral is made.

What is your experience of the referral pathway for corneal conditions? I am interested in whether the current system is identifying these patients early enough.

title: Multifocal lens implants: what patients are not always told before they decide
caption:
Multifocal intraocular lenses are one of the most significant advances in cataract surgery of the past twenty years. For appropriately selected patients, the chance of being completely glasses-free after surgery is real and the outcomes are excellent.

But I want to be direct about something that does not always get enough weight in patient-facing communications.

Multifocal lenses divide light. They create multiple focal points simultaneously. For most patients, the visual cortex adapts and the brain learns to select the relevant focus for the task. For some patients, it does not. Those patients experience halos, glare and reduced contrast sensitivity, particularly in low light. Night driving can become genuinely difficult.

The patient who asks me will I need glasses after my cataract surgery is asking the right question. But the question I need to answer first is are you the right patient for a lens that might eliminate glasses but might also change how you see at night.

That requires an honest conversation about lifestyle, expectations, driving habits, occupational visual demands and the clinical characteristics of their eye. It is not a five-minute decision.

At CES we take that conversation seriously because recommending a multifocal lens to the wrong patient creates a problem that is very difficult to reverse.

What do you observe in patients who have had multifocal lenses fitted elsewhere and are presenting with visual complaints? I am interested in whether this is a pattern others are seeing.
linkedInHook: Multifocal intraocular lenses are one of the most significant advances in cataract surgery of the past twenty years.

title: What dry AMD patients are not being told
caption:
When a patient receives a dry AMD diagnosis, the standard conversation has historically covered three things: lifestyle modification, AREDS supplementation and monitoring.

That conversation is not wrong. But I think it has become a ceiling that stops both clinicians and patients from asking the next question.

Dry AMD is not a static condition. It progresses. And for patients who are watching their central vision slowly narrow over years, being told there is nothing to treat is a significant burden to carry.

I have spent a considerable part of my career in medical retina, and I am cautious about overclaiming for emerging treatments. The evidence base for some newer approaches is still developing. But I also think patients have a right to know that the picture is more complex than monitor and wait.

If you have patients with early to intermediate dry AMD who have been told there are no options, a conversation with a medical retina specialist who is familiar with the full range of current approaches is worth having. Not because every patient will be a candidate for something different. But because some will be, and they deserve to know.

I am not making specific treatment claims in a LinkedIn post. What I am saying is that the referral threshold for a specialist opinion on dry AMD should be lower than it currently is in many settings.

I see patients with dry AMD at Headcorn, Southborough, Chatham and Pantiles as part of CES Medical.

Has this come up in your practice? I would be interested in whether GPs and optometrists feel they have enough information to have this conversation with their patients.
linkedInHook: When a patient receives a dry AMD diagnosis, the standard conversation has historically covered three things.

title: What the revalidation process for ophthalmologists actually involves
caption:
I served as Co-Lead for Revalidation at the Royal College of Ophthalmologists for four years. I want to explain what revalidation actually involves because I think patients and referring clinicians often have a vague sense of it as a box-ticking exercise. It is not.

Every consultant in the UK undergoes annual appraisal and five-yearly revalidation. To revalidate, a consultant must demonstrate: continuing professional development across the breadth of their practice, clinical outcome data, multi-source feedback from colleagues and patients, a record of significant events and complaints with evidence of reflection and learning and confirmation that they have no health or conduct issues affecting their practice.

The appraisal process requires a consultant to sit with an appraiser and work through all of this documentation in detail. It is not a rubber stamp.

When I held this role at the Royal College, I was responsible, alongside my co-lead, for ensuring that the standards applied to ophthalmologists across the country were meaningful. That experience changed how I think about my own practice. You cannot set rigorous standards for others and then hold yourself to a lower bar.

When a patient asks me how they know their surgeon is safe to practise, revalidation is part of the answer. Not the whole answer. But a real and important part.

Does the revalidation process come up in conversations with patients when they are choosing a provider? I am curious whether this is something patients are beginning to ask about directly.

title: Retinal detachment: the window that most patients do not know exists
caption:
The visual outcome from retinal detachment surgery is substantially better when the macula is still attached at the time of the operation.

Once the detachment reaches the macula, the central vision is affected. Surgery can still repair the retina, but the recovery of central vision is less predictable and often incomplete.

The challenge is that patients experiencing the warning signs of retinal detachment frequently do not understand the urgency. A sudden shower of floaters and flashes is frightening, but it does not feel like an emergency in the way that chest pain does. People wait to see if it settles. They book an appointment with their optician. They look it up online.

The signs that need same-day assessment: a sudden significant increase in floaters, flashes of light particularly in peripheral vision, or a shadow or curtain moving across the visual field. Any of these, particularly in combination, need to be assessed urgently. Not in a few days. That day.

For GPs: a patient presenting with these symptoms should be directed to an eye casualty department immediately, not referred in the routine pathway.

I am one of a small number of vitreoretinal surgeons in Kent. I see the outcomes difference that speed makes. It is real and it is significant.

What is your experience of patients presenting late with retinal symptoms? I would like to understand where the delay most commonly occurs.
linkedInHook: The visual outcome from retinal detachment surgery is substantially better when the macula is still attached at the time of the operation.

title: What a vitreoretinal surgeon actually does and why having one in Kent matters
caption:
Vitreoretinal surgery covers the back of the eye: the retina, the vitreous gel and the structures that support them.

The conditions that fall within this subspecialty include retinal detachment, macular hole, epiretinal membrane, vitreous haemorrhage, diabetic vitreoretinal disease and some forms of trauma. These are not common conditions in absolute terms. But when they occur, they are often sight-threatening and time-sensitive.

Vitreoretinal surgery requires subspecialty training beyond general ophthalmology. I completed an 18-month fellowship at Moorfields Eye Hospital specifically in this area, where the volume and complexity of cases provides an exposure that is difficult to replicate elsewhere.

The reason this matters geographically is that vitreoretinal surgeons are not evenly distributed. In many parts of the country, patients needing this surgery travel significant distances or wait for capacity at major centres. For time-sensitive conditions like retinal detachment, that waiting is not always clinically safe.

Being based in Kent with this subspecialty means that patients in Southborough, Chatham and across the region have access to this level of care closer to home.

If you have patients you are uncertain about referring, or conditions that you think might benefit from a vitreoretinal opinion, I am happy to discuss informally before a formal referral is made.

What is your current pathway for suspected retinal conditions? I would like to understand what the referral experience looks like from a GP or optometrist perspective in Kent.

title: Keratoconus: the early signs, and why they are easy to miss
caption:
Keratoconus is one of those conditions where the earlier we catch it, the more we can do, and where the early signs are genuinely easy to miss.

It usually appears in the teens or twenties. The cornea gradually thins and steepens, distorting vision. In the early stages it can look like an ordinary refractive change, which is exactly what makes it easy to overlook.

The signs worth a second thought: a prescription that keeps changing, particularly increasing or irregular astigmatism. Best-corrected acuity that is not quite as good as it should be. Subtle asymmetry between the two eyes. None of these is dramatic on its own, and that is the point.

Optometrists are the first line here. You see these patients regularly and you see their refractive changes over time, which is precisely the vantage point from which early keratoconus reveals itself. When something does not quite add up, corneal topography is the next step.

Why it matters so much: in early keratoconus, corneal cross-linking can halt progression. Caught early, we can often preserve the cornea the patient has. Caught late, the options narrow, and for some patients that eventually means a corneal transplant. The earlier the referral, the more of the patient's own cornea we can protect.

If you have a patient whose refractive picture is not behaving as expected, I am happy to discuss before a formal referral.

What makes you reach for topography in a younger patient with shifting astigmatism? I am interested in how early the suspicion tends to form in practice.
linkedInHook: Keratoconus is one of those conditions where the earlier we catch it, the more we can do, and where the early signs are genuinely easy to miss.

title: Vitreous floaters: when reassurance is right, and when it is not
caption:
Floaters are one of the most common things patients worry about, and one of the trickiest to advise on, because most of the time they are nothing, and occasionally they are the first sign of something serious.

Most floaters are harmless. They are usually caused by age-related changes in the vitreous gel inside the eye, including posterior vitreous detachment, which is a normal part of getting older. If they have been stable for a long time and there are no other symptoms, reassurance is usually the right answer.

What changes the picture is a sudden change. A sudden shower of new floaters. Flashes of light. A shadow or curtain moving across the vision. Any of those, particularly together, can signal a retinal tear or detachment, and that is not something to reassure away. It needs same-day assessment.

The difficulty is that the symptom itself, floaters, is the same in both cases. The history is what separates them. How suddenly did they appear? Are there flashes? Is there a shadow? Those questions matter more than the floaters themselves.

For anyone unsure: a sudden change with flashes or a shadow should be treated as urgent and assessed the same day, through eye casualty, not left for a routine appointment. When in doubt, it is safer to check.

For colleagues: how do you talk patients through the difference between the floaters that need watching and the ones that need acting on?
linkedInHook: Floaters are one of the most common things patients worry about. Most are harmless. Some are the first sign of something serious. The history is what separates them.

title: The moment you realise your vision has changed
caption:
There is usually a specific moment when it becomes undeniable.

For some people it is a car journey at night when the oncoming headlights suddenly seem unbearable. For others it is trying to read a menu in a restaurant and holding it further and further away. For others it is a grandchild pointing at something in the distance that they simply cannot see.

These moments are not signs of getting old. They are often signs of a cataract developing.

Cataracts are gradual. They are also very treatable. If you have had one of those moments recently, it is worth a conversation.

Call us on 01732 757771. No obligation, no rush.

Have you had a moment like this? We would love to hear from you in the comments.

title: What surgery day actually looks like
caption:
We hear the same fears every week.

"Will I be awake?" Yes, but patients do not feel pain. Local anaesthetic drops are used throughout.
"Will I see what is happening?" No. Patients see light and movement but not the procedure itself.
"What if I move my eye?" The team is trained for this. It is more common than you think and it does not affect the outcome.
"How long does it take?" Around 15 to 20 minutes per eye.
"Can my partner come?" Yes. They can wait in our reception and be with you immediately after.

Surgery on your eye is frightening to think about. Most patients tell us it was much more manageable than they expected.

If you have a question that is keeping you from making the first call, ask us.

Call 01732 757771 or comment below and we will come back to you.

title: Five signs your parent might have a cataract they have not mentioned
caption:
If your mum or dad is over 65, there is a reasonable chance they have a cataract developing that they have not told you about. Not because they are hiding it. Because it happened so gradually they did not notice.

Here are five things to look for.

Swipe through and save this one.
notes:

This is the highest share-potential post in the campaign. The fifth sign (reading habits) was added to fulfil the "five signs" promise in the title. Do not reduce back to four slides. "Save this one" in the caption is a save prompt, not engagement bait.

Slide 1: Five signs your parent might have a cataract they have not mentioned.
Slide 2: They have stopped driving at night. They probably said it is a preference. It may not be.
Slide 3: The television is louder than it used to be. When vision becomes difficult, people often compensate in other ways without realising it.
Slide 4: They are holding things further away to read. This is often mistaken for a reading glasses issue. It can be both.
Slide 5: They have stopped reading for pleasure or switched to large-print books. Patients often attribute this to preference rather than difficulty. It is worth asking about.
Slide 6: They seem less confident in unfamiliar places. Reduced contrast sensitivity from a cataract affects how clearly the world appears, making navigation harder. This is often attributed to age.
Slide 7: A simple eye test at their optician will show whether a cataract is developing. If it is, call us on 01732 757771 and our patient coordinator will help you find the right next step for them.

title: Drooping eyelids and vision
caption:
A drooping upper eyelid is not always a cosmetic concern.

When the lid drops far enough to cut into your field of vision, it becomes a medical condition. Patients describe it as: feeling like they are always looking from under a heavy brow, difficulty reading for long periods, needing to lift their chin to see clearly.

If this sounds familiar, it is worth a specialist assessment. In many cases it is covered by private medical insurance.

Mr Nick Kopsachilis, our oculoplastic specialist, sees patients at Tunbridge Wells and Headcorn. Call 01732 757771 to find out whether your eyelid condition is functional or cosmetic.

title: What happens after cataract surgery

Slide 2: Day 1. Your eye may feel gritty or scratchy. Vision may be blurry. This is normal. Rest at home. Use your drops as instructed.

title: Glaucoma and family history
caption:
If one of your parents had glaucoma, your risk of developing it is three to four times higher than average.

Glaucoma has no symptoms in its early stages. It damages your peripheral vision slowly, over years, without pain. By the time most people notice something is wrong, some damage has already occurred.

The good news is that caught early, glaucoma is very manageable. Drops, laser treatment and surgery are all available depending on the stage and type.

If you have a family history of glaucoma and have not had a full eye examination in the past two years, that is where to start.

Your optician can screen for early signs. If they refer you to us, we will see you promptly.

Call 01732 757771.

The good news is that caught early, glaucoma is very manageable. Drops, laser treatment and surgery are all available depending on the stage and type.

If you have a family history of glaucoma and have not had a full eye examination in the past two years, that is where to start.

Your optician can screen for early signs. If they refer you to us, we will see you promptly.

title: Lens options for cataract surgery: the honest comparison
caption:
Monofocal. Multifocal. EDOF. Toric.

If you are researching cataract surgery, the lens conversation can feel overwhelming. Here is an honest breakdown. Save this before your consultation.
notes:
Carousel. 7 slides. Instagram. High save potential. Designed to be returned to before a consultation.

Slide 1: Which cataract lens is right for you? The honest breakdown.

Slide 2: Monofocal lens. Corrects one distance, usually distance vision. Most patients still need reading glasses for close-up tasks. Some patients choose near focus depending on their occupation. The most established option. Included in standard cataract surgery.

Slide 3: Multifocal lens. Corrects multiple distances. Many patients find they need glasses far less or not at all. Not for everyone: some experience haloes or glare at night. Requires clinical assessment to confirm suitability.

Slide 4: EDOF (Extended Depth of Focus). Extended intermediate range. Fewer haloes than full multifocal. May still need reading glasses for fine print. A middle-ground option for many patients.

Slide 5: Toric lens. Corrects distance vision and astigmatism at the same time. Astigmatism is a common condition where the cornea is slightly irregular, causing blurry or distorted vision. A toric design can also be combined with multifocal technology for patients who want to correct both astigmatism and reading distance. Your consultant will advise at assessment.

Slide 6: Multifocal vs varifocal glasses. Not the same thing. A multifocal IOL is a surgical implant inside the eye. A varifocal is spectacle lenses. Confusing the two is common. A varifocal prescription will not replace a multifocal IOL, and vice versa. Your consultant will explain what combination suits you.

Slide 7: The right lens is the one that fits your life, not the one that looks best on a chart. That conversation happens at your consultation. cespatientinformation.co.uk

title: Glaucoma: everything you need to know if you have just been diagnosed
caption:
If you or someone you love has just been told they have glaucoma, this is for you.

It is a condition that sounds frightening and is frequently misunderstood. Swipe through for what it actually means, what the options are and what life with managed glaucoma looks like.

Save this.
notes:
Carousel. 9 slides. Instagram. High save and share potential. Designed to be shared by newly diagnosed patients with family members. Warm but authoritative. No alarm, no overclaiming on outcomes.

PDF or image carousel. If posted to LinkedIn as well, no video, so LinkedIn-compatible as a document carousel.

Slide 1: Just diagnosed with glaucoma? Here is what it actually means.

Slide 2: Glaucoma is not one condition. It is a group of conditions that damage the optic nerve, usually through raised pressure inside the eye.

Slide 3: The most important thing to know: caught early, glaucoma is very manageable. The damage cannot be reversed but it can be slowed significantly.

Slide 4: Why you may not have noticed symptoms. Glaucoma typically affects peripheral vision first. The brain compensates so well that most people do not notice until a screening test picks it up.

Slide 5: What management looks like. Eye drops (most common first step). SLT laser (five-minute outpatient procedure, very effective at lowering pressure). Surgery for advanced cases.

Slide 6: About SLT laser at CES Medical. Selective Laser Trabeculoplasty is a quick, well-tolerated procedure that lowers eye pressure without daily drop compliance. It is increasingly offered as a first-line option.

Slide 7: What monitoring involves. Regular appointments to check eye pressure, optic nerve imaging and visual field testing. Usually every six to twelve months depending on stability.

Slide 8: Does it mean I will go blind? For most patients managed appropriately from diagnosis: no. Sight loss from glaucoma is largely preventable with treatment.

Slide 9: We see glaucoma patients at Chatham, Headcorn and Southborough. cespatientinformation.co.uk or call 01732 757771.

Tone: warm, authoritative, reassuring. This post is designed to be shared by a newly diagnosed patient with their worried family. Slide 8 directly answers the question they are all thinking.

title: Dry eye: why it is being under-treated
caption:
Dry eye is one of the most under-treated conditions in ophthalmology.

Not because it is rare. Because most people manage it with drops for years without knowing there are other options.

Swipe through for what we actually offer dry eye patients at CES Medical.
notes:
Carousel. 7 slides. Instagram. High save potential for patients who have been managing dry eye for years. Audience: adults 40 to 65 with persistent dry eye.

This is the Instagram companion to P139 (dry eye and IPL long-form video). Keep the MGD and IPL content consistent between them.

Visual: A close-up clinical image of a slit lamp examination can be used for visual credibility, but it must be non-alarming. Avoid graphic or distressing eye imagery. A clean, professional clinical setting image is preferable to an extreme close-up.

COMMERCIAL NOTE: IPL for dry eye is a private service outside NHS commissioning and a genuine CES differentiator. This carousel reaches the large dry eye audience who have used drops for years without knowing IPL exists.

Slide 1: Dry eye. More than just irritation.

Slide 2: What dry eye actually is. The eye does not produce enough tears or produces tears of insufficient quality. The result: irritation, redness, blurred vision and difficulty with screens, reading and driving.

Slide 3: Why drops alone are often not enough. Drops provide temporary relief by supplementing tears. They do not address the underlying cause, usually meibomian gland dysfunction (blocked glands at the eyelid margin).

Slide 4: IPL treatment for dry eye. Intense Pulsed Light therapy targets the meibomian glands directly, improving the quality of the oil layer of the tear film. Supported by clinical evidence. Available at CES Medical.

Slide 5: Who is a candidate for IPL? Patients who have been using drops for months or years without sustained relief. Patients with meibomian gland dysfunction confirmed on examination. Patients whose dry eye significantly affects screen work, driving or reading.

Slide 6: What IPL treatment involves. A series of sessions, typically three to four, spaced a few weeks apart. Non-invasive. No downtime. Many patients notice improvement during the course of treatment, though results vary.

Slide 7: If drops have not been enough, IPL is worth asking about. cespatientinformation.co.uk or call 01732 757771.

title: What does recovery from cataract surgery actually look like?
caption:
Day one to week six: the honest guide to cataract surgery recovery.

Most people are surprised by how manageable it is. Save this before your procedure.
notes:
Carousel. 8 slides. Instagram. Very high save potential. Audience: patients preparing for or considering cataract surgery. Warm teal design throughout.

The day-by-day structure is useful but must be framed as typical, not guaranteed. Recovery varies by patient, and the surgeon's individual advice takes precedence. Slides 1, 4 and 5 carry this framing.

Slide 8 patient quote (I wish I had done it sooner) is a general patient sentiment, not an attributed testimonial with a health outcome claim, which keeps it within ASA compliance. This is acceptable as worded. Keep.

VERIFY BEFORE PUBLISHING: The 24-hour post-operative helpline (01732 756789) on Slide 7 must be confirmed active and staffed 24 hours. Correct the claim if it is not.

Slide 1: Cataract surgery recovery. What typically happens. Your surgeon will advise on your individual recovery.

Slide 2: Day 1. Eye may feel gritty or scratchy. Vision may be blurry. This is normal. Rest at home. Use your drops as instructed. Do not rub the eye.

Slide 3: Days 2 to 4. Vision usually begins to settle. Light sensitivity may continue. Gentle daily activity is fine.

Slide 4: Around day 7. Follow-up appointment with your surgeon. Vision assessed. Many patients are driving again around this time, once their surgeon confirms their vision meets the required standard.

Slide 5: Weeks 2 to 4. Avoid swimming and heavy lifting. Most people are back to their normal daily routine. Some light exercise is usually fine after the first week. Your surgeon will advise on your individual recovery.

Slide 6: Week 6. Final review appointment. The result has settled. Your surgeon will assess the outcome and discuss whether a second eye is needed.

Slide 7: Not sure if something is normal? Our 24-hour post-operative helpline: 01732 756789. Available from the day of your surgery.

Slide 8: The most common thing patients tell us after surgery: I wish I had done it sooner. cespatientinformation.co.uk

title: Corneal Transplant Surgery Explained | Types, Recovery and What to Expect | CES Medical Kent
caption:
A corneal transplant can restore sight when the cornea becomes damaged, scarred or clouded. But many patients do not know that modern corneal surgery is often partial, replacing only the affected layer rather than the whole cornea.

Mr Nick Kopsachilis, Consultant Ophthalmic Surgeon at CES Medical, explains what corneal transplantation involves, the different types of transplant, who they help and what recovery is really like.

Mr Kopsachilis holds an MD and a PhD in corneal research and trained in Munich, at Moorfields Eye Hospital and at King's College London. He leads the corneal service at CES Medical.

This video is for patients with conditions such as keratoconus, Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy or corneal scarring, and for anyone who has been told they may need a corneal transplant.

title: Dry Eye That Drops Will Not Fix? IPL Treatment Explained | CES Medical Kent
caption:
If you have been using eye drops for dry eye for months or years without lasting relief, the problem may not be a lack of tears. For many people, dry eye is caused by blocked oil glands in the eyelids, and drops alone cannot fix that.

Elion Hyseni, Consultant Clinical Optometrist and Founder of CES Medical, explains what really causes most dry eye, why drops are often not enough, and how Intense Pulsed Light (IPL) treatment works.

Dry eye is one of the most under-treated conditions in eye care. Not because it is rare, but because many people manage it with drops for years without knowing there are other options.

IPL is a private treatment available at CES Medical. It is not suitable for everyone, and we assess suitability at consultation.

CES Medical provides IPL treatment for dry eye in Kent.

To book an assessment: 01732 757771
Patient information: https://cespatientinformation.co.uk

9:30 Dry eye care at CES Medical
notes:
SCHEDULING NOTE: DATE DISCREPANCY. Two dates recorded: 2026-06-08 (rebalancing schedule) and 2026-06-19 (YouTube source file). This build uses 2026-06-08 as the current scheduled date. Confirm with the team before scheduling.

COMMERCIAL NOTE: IPL for dry eye is a private service outside NHS commissioning and a genuine CES differentiator. This video reaches the large dry eye audience who have managed with drops for years and do not know IPL exists. The goal is to reach those patients and convert them to a suitability assessment.

CLINICAL ACCURACY: Dry eye is frequently caused by meibomian gland dysfunction (blocked oil glands at the eyelid margin), not just insufficient tears. Drops provide temporary relief but do not address MGD. IPL targets the meibomian glands to improve the oil layer of the tear film. IPL is a series of sessions (typically three to four), non-invasive, no downtime. Must NOT overclaim: IPL helps many but not all dry eye patients, and suitability is assessed at consultation. The not suitable for everyone framing must remain.

Presenter note: Elion (Consultant Clinical Optometrist) is the appropriate presenter, dry eye and IPL are within optometric scope and he can speak to them credibly. Introduce by name and title at the start.

THUMBNAIL BRIEF: Elion Hyseni in clinical attire, warm and direct to camera. Text overlay in PP Telegraph white on teal bar: Dry eye drops not working? This is the question that stops the target audience scrolling. Face-first. No clinical close-up imagery of eyes, which can be off-putting. Capture a dedicated thumbnail shot on the filming day.

VIDEO STRUCTURE. Elion presents throughout. Clinical attire.

0:00 to 0:20: Elion introduction. I am Elion Hyseni, Consultant Clinical Optometrist and Founder of CES Medical.

0:20 to 1:30: The hook. Many people use dry eye drops for months or years without lasting relief, because drops treat the symptom, not the cause.

1:30 to 3:00: What dry eye actually is. Insufficient tears or poor quality tears. Symptoms: irritation, redness, blurred vision, difficulty with screens, reading and driving.

3:00 to 4:30: Meibomian gland dysfunction. The most common underlying cause. Blocked oil glands at the eyelid margin mean the tear film evaporates too quickly. Explain in plain English.

4:30 to 6:30: How IPL works. Intense Pulsed Light applied to the area around the eyes stimulates and unblocks the meibomian glands, improving the oil layer of the tear film. Clinically used for MGD-related dry eye.

6:30 to 8:00: Who it suits and who it does not. Suitable: patients with MGD-related dry eye who have not responded to drops. Not suitable for everyone, and certain skin types and conditions need assessment. Suitability is decided at consultation.

8:00 to 9:30: What the treatment involves. A series of sessions, typically three to four, spaced a few weeks apart. Non-invasive. No downtime. Many patients notice improvement after the second session, though results vary.

9:30 to 11:00: Dry eye care at CES Medical. Elion closing. If you have been managing dry eye with drops without lasting relief, an assessment can tell you whether IPL might help. Call us on 01732 757771.

COMMERCIAL AND COMPLIANCE NOTE: Premium lenses are where CES differentiates and where patient value is highest. This video supports the lens education funnel (P05, P08, P09, P14, P15, P58). It must lead with patient benefit but stay honest. Premium lenses are not right for everyone. The we will always tell you honestly which option suits you framing must remain. No outcome guarantees. Glasses-free must be phrased as need glasses far less or not at all. Over-selling premium lenses would breach ASA and GMC standards and damage trust.

CLINICAL ACCURACY: Explain each lens type and its trade-offs honestly. Multifocal can cause haloes and reduced contrast in some patients. EDOF extends intermediate range with fewer haloes than full multifocal but may still need reading glasses. Toric corrects astigmatism (explain astigmatism in plain English: a common condition where the cornea is slightly irregular, causing blurry or distorted vision). Toric can be combined with multifocal. Consistent with lens carousels P05, P08, P09, P15, P58.

0:20 to 1:30: Why lens choice matters. During cataract surgery the natural lens is replaced with an artificial one. That lens stays for life, so the choice shapes your vision permanently.

1:30 to 3:00: Monofocal. Corrects one distance, usually distance vision. Reading glasses still needed for close work. Reliable and well established. The NHS standard lens.

3:00 to 5:00: Multifocal. Vision at multiple distances. Many patients find they need glasses far less or not at all. Not for everyone, some notice haloes or reduced contrast in low light.

5:00 to 6:30: EDOF. Extended depth of focus, good intermediate range, fewer haloes than full multifocal, some may still need reading glasses.

title: Diabetic Retinopathy Explained: Screening, Grades and Treatment | CES Medical Kent
caption:
If you have diabetes, your eyes are at risk in a way that often has no symptoms until damage is done. Diabetic retinopathy is one of the leading causes of preventable sight loss in working-age adults, and screening is what catches it early.

Mr Syed Shahid, Consultant Ophthalmologist and Vitreoretinal Surgeon at CES Medical, explains what diabetic retinopathy is, why the NHS screening programme matters, what the screening grades mean, and how the condition is managed when treatment is needed.

Mr Shahid is the Clinical Lead for the Kent and Medway Diabetic Eye Screening Programme.

This video supports the NHS diabetic eye screening programme. If you have diabetes, attend your annual screening. It is the single most important thing you can do to protect your sight.

NHS SCREENING SUPPORT, CRITICAL: This video must reinforce the value of NHS diabetic eye screening and encourage attendance. It must NOT divert patients from NHS screening. CES is positioned as specialist management for referable disease (R2, R3, M1), working with the screening pathway, which Mr Shahid leads regionally. The attend your annual screening message must be prominent. Consistent with P79, P92 and the ICB relationship rule.

CLINICAL REVIEW REQUIRED: Mr Shahid should review the clinical content (grades, treatments) for accuracy before publishing.

CLINICAL ACCURACY: Diabetic retinopathy is damage to retinal blood vessels from diabetes. Symptomless in early stages. Screening grades R0 to R3 and M1 (consistent with P92). Management for referable disease: laser, intravitreal (IVT) injections, vitreoretinal surgery for advanced cases. Good glucose and blood pressure control slows progression at every stage. No outcome guarantees.

0:00 to 0:20: Introduction. I am Mr Syed Shahid, Consultant Ophthalmologist and Vitreoretinal Surgeon at CES Medical, and Clinical Lead for the Kent and Medway Diabetic Eye Screening Programme.

0:20 to 1:30: Why diabetes affects the eyes. High blood sugar damages the small blood vessels in the retina over time.

1:30 to 3:00: Why screening matters. Diabetic retinopathy is symptomless early on. Annual NHS screening catches it before sight is affected. Strong encouragement to attend.

3:00 to 5:30: What the grades mean. R0 no retinopathy, R1 background (common, monitored), R2 pre-proliferative, R3 proliferative, M1 maculopathy. Reassure that R0 and R1 are managed within routine screening.

5:30 to 7:00: When treatment is needed. Referable grades (R2, R3, M1) mean specialist assessment.

7:00 to 9:00: Laser and injection treatments. Laser for proliferative disease, intravitreal injections for maculopathy and proliferative disease. Explained in plain English.

9:00 to 10:30: Surgery for advanced disease. Vitreoretinal surgery for advanced complications such as vitreous haemorrhage or tractional detachment.

10:30 to 11:30: The role of diabetes control. Good glucose and blood pressure control slows progression at every stage. The patient's own management matters enormously.

11:30 to 13:00: Diabetic eye care at CES Medical. Mr Shahid closing. Attend your annual diabetic eye screening. If you are referred for specialist assessment, we are here. Call us on 01732 757771.

title: How a Routine Eye Test Caught My Glaucoma Early | Patient Story | CES Medical Kent
caption:
Glaucoma has no symptoms in the early stages. This patient had no idea anything was wrong until a routine eye test picked it up.

In this video they share what it was like to be diagnosed with glaucoma without any warning signs, what the treatment involved, and why they now encourage everyone they know to have regular eye tests.

Glaucoma is the leading preventable cause of sight loss in the UK. The damage it causes cannot be reversed, but when it is caught early it can be managed and further sight loss slowed. A routine eye test is the most reliable way to catch it before you notice anything.

If you have not had an eye test in the past two years, this is your reminder to book one.

CES Medical provides glaucoma diagnosis and management across Kent, including Chatham, Headcorn and Southborough.

title: What is YAG Capsulotomy? #Shorts
caption:
If your vision has gone blurry again after cataract surgery, your cataract has not come back. Cataracts cannot return after surgery. What has happened is the membrane behind your lens implant has become cloudy. This is called posterior capsule opacification and it is very common.

YAG capsulotomy is a simple five-minute laser procedure that clears the cloudiness. No incision. No stitches. Most patients notice improved vision the same day.

If your vision has deteriorated after cataract surgery, call us on 01732 757771. Mr Kashif Qureshi, our medical retina and cataract specialist, explains what YAG capsulotomy involves.

CES Medical provides YAG capsulotomy at Chatham, Headcorn and Southborough.

title: Warning Signs of Retinal Detachment #Shorts
caption:
Retinal detachment is a medical emergency. If it is treated quickly, sight can often be saved. If it is left, it can cause permanent sight loss.

Know the three warning signs:
A sudden increase in floaters.
Flashes of light in your vision.
A dark shadow or curtain spreading across your sight.

If you experience any of these, do not wait. Go to your nearest eye casualty department or call 111 the same day. This is not something to leave until morning or until your next optician appointment.

Mr Syed Shahid, Consultant Ophthalmologist and Vitreoretinal Surgeon at CES Medical, explains the warning signs every person should know.

CES Medical provides vitreoretinal surgery at Southborough. For non-urgent retinal concerns: 01732 757771. For urgent symptoms, go to eye casualty or call 111.

cta.label: For urgent symptoms: eye casualty or 111

notes:

TONE: Urgent and serious throughout. This Short is the tonal opposite of P152 (YAG capsulotomy), which is calm and reassuring. Do not confuse the two. P152 reassures. P153 warns. The gravity of P153 is intentional and must not be softened.

CRITICAL CALL TO ACTION: The CTA is NOT "call CES Medical to book." Retinal detachment is an emergency requiring immediate NHS eye casualty care. The CTA is "go to eye casualty or call 111 the same day." CES Medical is credited as the source of the information through Mr Shahid, but the action directed to the viewer is urgent care, not a private booking. Under no circumstances should this Short direct a person with active symptoms to a booking line instead of emergency care. This is a patient safety requirement.

title: Glaucoma Has No Symptoms. Here Is Why That Matters. #Shorts
caption:
Glaucoma is one of the leading causes of preventable sight loss in the UK, and in its early stages it has no symptoms at all. No pain, no blurring, no warning. The damage it causes cannot be reversed, but caught early it can be managed and further sight loss slowed.

The only reliable way to catch glaucoma early is a routine eye test, even when your vision feels completely normal.

If you have not had an eye test in the past two years, book one. It is the single most useful thing you can do to protect your sight.

CES Medical provides glaucoma diagnosis and management across Kent.

To find out more: 01732 757771
Patient information: https://cespatientinformation.co.uk/glaucoma
notes:
YouTube Short. 45 to 55 seconds. Brand content, motion design with on-screen text. Subtitled throughout.

This is the distilled glaucoma awareness Short. It pairs with P62 (Instagram carousel), P85 (LinkedIn), P136 (long-form explainer) and P149 (patient story). Top-of-funnel awareness.

TONE: Calm and clear, with genuine importance. Not alarming, but not casual. This is public health get tested messaging. The fact that glaucoma is symptomless is the hook, and the action is a routine eye test.

CLINICAL ACCURACY: Glaucoma damages peripheral vision first (correct for glaucoma, do not change to central). No symptoms or pain in early stages. The brain compensates for early peripheral loss, which is why it goes unnoticed. Damage is irreversible but progression can be slowed if caught early.

CTA, IMPORTANT: Glaucoma is not an emergency. The action is a routine eye test (at an optician or CES), NOT eye casualty. This is different from the retinal detachment Short (P153), which routes to urgent care. Here the message is book a routine eye test. Public health first.

ON-SCREEN TEXT CARDS:

Card 1 (0:00 to 0:08): Glaucoma has no symptoms in its early stages. / No pain. No blurring. No warning.

Card 2 (0:08 to 0:17): It slowly damages your peripheral vision first. / Your brain compensates, so you do not notice.

Card 3 (0:17 to 0:26): By the time most people notice, some sight has already been lost. / And that loss cannot be reversed.

Card 4 (0:26 to 0:35): But caught early, glaucoma can be managed. / Further sight loss can be slowed.

Card 5 (0:35 to 0:44): The only reliable way to catch it early is a routine eye test. / Even when your vision feels normal.

Card 6 (0:44 to 0:50): Not had an eye test in two years? Book one. It protects your sight.

title: Is Your Drooping Eyelid a Medical Condition? #Shorts
caption:
Many people assume a drooping eyelid is just a cosmetic issue. For some, it is. But when a drooping upper eyelid starts to block the upper part of your vision, it becomes a medical condition, and that often changes everything, including whether treatment may be covered by insurance.

Mr Nick Kopsachilis, our oculoplastic specialist at CES Medical, explains the difference.

CES Medical provides oculoplastic surgery at Headcorn and Chatham.

To find out more: 01732 757771
Patient information: https://cespatientinformation.co.uk
notes:
YouTube Short. 45 to 55 seconds. Vertical format 9:16. Subtitled throughout. No watermarks. Source: NK-C6 interview clip with Mr Nick Kopsachilis, or motion design using the on-screen text sequence.

This is the Short version of the functional-vs-cosmetic eyelid message, the highest-value point in the oculoplastic content (P10, P75, P80, P91, P148, P61). Keep consistent.

CLINICAL ACCURACY: A drooping upper eyelid (ptosis) that affects the UPPER FIELD OF VISION is a functional medical condition. Use "field of vision" or "upper field of vision," NOT "peripheral vision" (peripheral vision loss is a glaucoma marker, not ptosis). The functional vs cosmetic distinction determines the pathway and often insurance coverage. Suitability and the functional/cosmetic determination are assessed at consultation.

TONE: Calm, informative, empowering. Like the calm Shorts (P152, P155, P157, P158). The hook is the question, prompting self-recognition. No pressure.

title: Retinal detachment: warning signs you should know
caption:
A sudden increase in floaters. Flashes of light at the edge of your vision. A shadow or curtain moving across your sight.

These are the warning signs of retinal detachment. It is a medical emergency. Do not wait.

If you or someone you know experiences these symptoms, go to your nearest eye casualty department or call 111 the same day. This is not something to leave until morning or until a routine appointment.

Mr Syed Shahid, our Moorfields-trained vitreoretinal surgeon, is one of a small number of vitreoretinal surgeons in Kent. For non-urgent retinal concerns or follow-up, call us on 01732 757771.

title: Keratoconus: what it is and when to seek help
caption:
Keratoconus is a progressive condition where the cornea gradually thins and bulges forward. It typically starts in the teens or twenties and can significantly affect vision if left unmanaged.

The good news: caught early, it is very treatable. Corneal crosslinking can halt progression. Advanced cases may need a corneal transplant, a procedure Mr Nick Kopsachilis performs at Headcorn.

If your glasses prescription keeps changing or your vision is distorted even with correction, it is worth a specialist assessment.

Visit cespatientinformation.co.uk or call 01732 757771.
notes:
Carousel 6 slides. Slide 1: What is keratoconus? Slide 2: The cornea is the clear window at the front of the eye. In keratoconus it gradually thins and changes shape, distorting vision in a way that glasses and contact lenses cannot fully correct. Slide 3: Who does it affect? Most commonly people in their teens and twenties. Often hereditary. More common in patients who rub their eyes frequently. Slide 4: How is it managed? Corneal crosslinking strengthens the cornea and halts progression. It works best when caught early. Slide 5: When is a corneal transplant needed? In advanced cases where the cornea is significantly misshapen, a transplant may be the right option. Mr Kopsachilis performs DMEK and other transplant procedures at Headcorn. Slide 6: If your vision keeps changing despite updated glasses, ask your optician to check for keratoconus. Early referral makes a significant difference. Visit cespatientinformation.co.uk or call 01732 757771.

FRCOphth: Mr Kopsachilis holds MD, PhD, NOT FRCOphth. Do not tag or describe with FRCOphth.

LOCATION: Corneal surgery is at Headcorn, Chatham and Southborough, not Pantiles. Transplants at Headcorn confirmed.

COLLEGIAL: Collegial toward optometrists, frame early referral as them being well-placed to catch it, not missing it. Consistent with P101, P132.

CLINICAL REVIEW: Required before publishing. Mr Kopsachilis to review. No graphic surgical imagery. DSEK/DMEK terminology: use DMEK, DSAEK, DALK consistently.

title: Dry eye and IPL: when drops are not enough
caption:
If you have been using dry eye drops for months without lasting relief, you may not be getting to the cause.

Most persistent dry eye is caused by meibomian gland dysfunction, blocked glands at the eyelid margin that affect the oil layer of your tears. Drops supplement the tears. IPL treats the glands directly.

We offer Intense Pulsed Light treatment for dry eye at CES Medical. It is non-invasive, takes around 15 minutes per session and most patients need three to four sessions spaced a few weeks apart.

Call 01732 757771 to ask whether IPL is suitable for you.
notes:
Carousel 6 slides. Slide 1: Dry eye and IPL: when drops are not enough. Slide 2: Persistent dry eye is rarely just about tear volume. The most common cause is meibomian gland dysfunction, blocked glands that affect the quality of the oil layer of your tears. Slide 3: What drops do. They supplement the tears and provide temporary relief. They do not address the blocked glands that cause the problem. Slide 4: What IPL does. Intense Pulsed Light targets the meibomian glands directly, improving the oil layer and reducing the cycle of inflammation. Supported by clinical evidence. Non-invasive. Slide 5: Who it suits. Patients who have been using drops for months or years without sustained improvement. Patients with confirmed meibomian gland dysfunction. Patients whose dry eye significantly affects screen work, driving or reading. Slide 6: Three to four sessions, spaced a few weeks apart. Many patients notice improvement during the course of treatment, though results vary. Call 01732 757771 to find out if IPL is right for you.

IPL is a private service outside NHS commissioning, a genuine CES differentiator. Consistent with P23, P64, P93, P139. Use "supported by clinical evidence," not "clinically proven." Not suitable for everyone, assessed at consultation.
